cards.title: First Aid — Primary Survey (ABCDE Approach)

alerts.label: ⚠️ CRITICAL — Primary Survey Must Be Completed Before Any Other Assessment
alerts.text: The ABCDE approach ensures life-threatening conditions are identified and managed systematically in order of priority. Do not proceed to the next step until the current one is stabilised. Call emergency services (108 in India) simultaneously when life-threatening conditions identified.

steps.title: Airway
steps.text: Is the airway patent? Look for: chest rise, listen for breathing sounds, feel for air movement. Obstruction signs: stridor (partial), silence (complete), snoring (soft tissue). Interventions: jaw thrust (trauma — spinal precaution), chin lift (no trauma), head-tilt chin-lift, suction secretions, oropharyngeal airway (Guedel — only if unconscious), nasopharyngeal airway (conscious/semi-conscious, no basal skull fracture). Cervical spine immobilisation if trauma suspected.

steps.title: Breathing
steps.text: Assess: RR (normal 12–20/min), SpO₂ (normal ≥95%), chest expansion symmetry, work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring, paradoxical breathing), percussion (dullness = haemothorax/effusion; hyper-resonance = pneumothorax), auscultation (air entry, wheeze, crackles). Emergencies: tension pneumothorax (deviated trachea, absent breath sounds, haemodynamic compromise — needle decompression 2nd ICS MCL immediately). Supplemental oxygen 15L/min via non-rebreathe mask if respiratory distress.

steps.title: Circulation
steps.text: Assess: pulse rate/rhythm/quality (radial absent → BP <80 — carotid/femoral). Cap refill (normal <2 sec). BP. Skin: pale/mottled/clammy = shock. JVP (raised in cardiac tamponade, tension pneumo, right heart failure). Major haemorrhage: direct pressure — NEVER remove soaked dressing (add more on top). Tourniquet for limb haemorrhage — note time of application. IV access × 2 large bore if available and trained.

steps.title: Disability (Neurological)
steps.text: GCS (Eyes 1–4, Voice 1–5, Motor 1–6 — max 15. GCS ≤8 = severe TBI — airway at risk). AVPU: Alert, Voice response, Pain response, Unresponsive. Pupils: size, equality, reactivity (blown pupil = CN III compression — uncal herniation — neurosurgical emergency). Blood glucose: hypoglycaemia mimics stroke, confusion, seizure — check ALWAYS. Seizure: protect from injury, lateral position, time the seizure, do NOT restrain or put anything in mouth.

steps.title: Exposure & Environment
steps.text: Remove clothing to expose completely — do not miss hidden injuries (especially back and perineum in trauma). Log roll to examine back (minimum 3 people for spinal precaution). Maintain patient dignity throughout. Prevent hypothermia — use warming blanket after examination. Document all findings systematically. Reassess ABCDE continuously — deterioration can be rapid.

cards._legacyHtml:
⚠️ CRITICAL — Primary Survey Must Be Completed Before Any Other Assessment
The ABCDE approach ensures life-threatening conditions are identified and managed systematically in order of priority. Do not proceed to the next step until the current one is stabilised. Call emergency services (108 in India) simultaneously when life-threatening conditions identified.
A
Airway Is the airway patent? Look for: chest rise, listen for breathing sounds, feel for air movement. Obstruction signs: stridor (partial), silence (complete), snoring (soft tissue). Interventions: jaw thrust (trauma — spinal precaution), chin lift (no trauma), head-tilt chin-lift, suction secretions, oropharyngeal airway (Guedel — only if unconscious), nasopharyngeal airway (conscious/semi-conscious, no basal skull fracture). Cervical spine immobilisation if trauma suspected.
B
Breathing Assess: RR (normal 12–20/min), SpO₂ (normal ≥95%), chest expansion symmetry, work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring, paradoxical breathing), percussion (dullness = haemothorax/effusion; hyper-resonance = pneumothorax), auscultation (air entry, wheeze, crackles). Emergencies: tension pneumothorax (deviated trachea, absent breath sounds, haemodynamic compromise — needle decompression 2nd ICS MCL immediately). Supplemental oxygen 15L/min via non-rebreathe mask if respiratory distress.
C
Circulation Assess: pulse rate/rhythm/quality (radial absent → BP <80 — carotid/femoral). Cap refill (normal <2 sec). BP. Skin: pale/mottled/clammy = shock. JVP (raised in cardiac tamponade, tension pneumo, right heart failure). Major haemorrhage: direct pressure — NEVER remove soaked dressing (add more on top). Tourniquet for limb haemorrhage — note time of application. IV access × 2 large bore if available and trained.
D
Disability (Neurological) GCS (Eyes 1–4, Voice 1–5, Motor 1–6 — max 15. GCS ≤8 = severe TBI — airway at risk). AVPU: Alert, Voice response, Pain response, Unresponsive. Pupils: size, equality, reactivity (blown pupil = CN III compression — uncal herniation — neurosurgical emergency). Blood glucose: hypoglycaemia mimics stroke, confusion, seizure — check ALWAYS. Seizure: protect from injury, lateral position, time the seizure, do NOT restrain or put anything in mouth.
E
Exposure & Environment Remove clothing to expose completely — do not miss hidden injuries (especially back and perineum in trauma). Log roll to examine back (minimum 3 people for spinal precaution). Maintain patient dignity throughout. Prevent hypothermia — use warming blanket after examination. Document all findings systematically. Reassess ABCDE continuously — deterioration can be rapid.

cards.title: Basic Life Support (BLS) — 2021 Guidelines

alerts.label: Chain of Survival — Each Link Is Critical
alerts.text: Early recognition → Call EMS (108) → High-quality CPR → Defibrillation → Advanced care → Post-arrest care

rows: Child (1–8 years)
1 hand or 2-finger technique. Depth: 4cm. Rate: 100–120/min. 30:2 ratio. Paediatric AED pads/attenuation key if available
Infant (<1 year)
2-finger technique on sternum below nipple line. Depth: 4cm. 15:2 ratio (2 rescuers). Mouth + nose seal for rescue breaths
Drowning
5 rescue breaths FIRST before compressions (hypoxic arrest — oxygenation priority different from cardiac VF)
Pregnant (>20 weeks)
Manual left uterine displacement during CPR. Do not delay defibrillation. Emergency caesarean (perimortem C-section) within 4–5 min if arrest not reversed — improves maternal AND foetal outcome

steps.title: Safety & Recognition
steps.text: Ensure scene safety. Approach safely. Check response: shout "Are you OK?" + tap shoulders firmly. Unresponsive + no normal breathing (gasping/agonal breathing counts as no breathing) = CARDIAC ARREST. Shout for help. Send bystander for AED. Call 108 yourself if alone (speakerphone). Do NOT leave patient to call — use mobile.

steps.title: Chest Compressions — QUALITY IS EVERYTHING
steps.text: Rate: 100–120/min (Stayin' Alive tempo). Depth: 5–6cm (2 inches) — allow FULL recoil between compressions (do not lean on chest). Position: heel of hand on lower half of sternum, other hand interlaced on top, arms straight. Minimise interruptions — aim for ≥80% chest compression fraction. Rotate every 2 min if possible (compressor fatigue degrades quality after 2 min).

steps.title: Rescue Breaths (if trained)
steps.text: 30 compressions : 2 breaths. Head-tilt chin-lift. Seal around mouth, pinch nose. Breathe over 1 second — see chest rise. 2 breaths should take no more than 10 seconds. If reluctant/untrained: compression-only CPR is acceptable and effective. Compression-only CPR (no breaths) for first 10 min = nearly equivalent outcomes.

steps.title: AED — Use as Soon as Available
steps.text: Turn on → follow voice prompts → place pads (right subclavicular, left lateral chest V5 position) → ensure no one touching patient → analyse → shock if advised → IMMEDIATELY resume CPR for 2 min → reanalyse. Every minute of delay to defibrillation = 10% reduction in survival. AED use with CPR = 70% survival in witnessed VF (vs 5% CPR alone).

Rotate every 2 min if possible (compressor fatigue degrades quality after 2 min).

Every minute of delay to defibrillation = 10% reduction in survival. AED use with CPR = 70% survival in witnessed VF (vs 5% CPR alone).
SituationModification
Child (1–8 years)1 hand or 2-finger technique. Depth: 4cm. Rate: 100–120/min. 30:2 ratio. Paediatric AED pads/attenuation key if available
Infant (<1 year)2-finger technique on sternum below nipple line. Depth: 4cm. 15:2 ratio (2 rescuers). Mouth + nose seal for rescue breaths

cards.title: Common First Aid Emergencies — Quick Reference

rows: Anaphylaxis
Urticaria + angioedema + bronchospasm/stridor + hypotension after trigger (food, bee, drug)
Adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.5mg IM anterolateral thigh (adults). Supine + legs elevated. O₂ 15L. Call 108. Second dose at 5 min if no improvement. Antihistamine AFTER adrenaline (not instead of)
ALL anaphylaxis — 108 immediately. Biphasic reactions occur 1–72hrs after apparent recovery — minimum 4–6hr observation in hospital
Choking (adult)
Universal choking sign, cannot speak/breathe, cyanosis
5 back blows (heel of hand between scapulae, lean forward). 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich). Alternate until cleared or unconscious. Unconscious → CPR (each compression attempt may dislodge)
Any persistent respiratory distress after clearing. Oesophageal impaction after episode.
Hypoglycaemia
Sweating, tremor, confusion, pallor in diabetic patient (or any confused patient)

Conscious: 15–20g fast glucose (glucose tabs, 150ml fruit juice, 3–4 teaspoons sugar). Recheck after 15 min — repeat if still <4mmol/L. Unconscious/cannot swallow: DO NOT give oral glucose (aspiration risk) → glucagon 1mg IM (if available) or 108 for IV dextrose
GCS ≤8, persistent hypoglycaemia, no response to glucagon, on sulphonylurea (prolonged action — need IV glucose infusion)
Seizure
Tonic-clonic jerking, loss of consciousness, incontinence

Protect from injury (pad environment, nothing in mouth). Lateral recovery position post-ictal. Time the seizure. DO NOT restrain. Most stop within 2–3 min. Post-seizure: drowsy, confused = normal (Todd's paresis may occur)
First seizure ever. Duration >5 min (status epilepticus). No recovery between seizures. Head injury before seizure. Hypoglycaemia cause. Call 108 for all status epilepticus.
Burns
Superficial: red, pain. Partial thickness: blisters, pain. Full thickness: white/brown, painless (nerve destruction)

Cool running water 20 min (NOT ice — vasoconstriction worsens). Cover with cling film (burns kitchen) or non-adherent dressing. Analgesia. Do NOT burst blisters. Do NOT apply toothpaste/butter/oils. Do NOT remove adherent clothing
>1% BSA in infant, >3% in child, >5% in adult. Face, hands, genitalia, circumferential burns. Inhalation injury. Chemical/electrical burns. All full-thickness burns.
Stroke (FAST)
Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech disturbance, Time to call 108
Lay patient down with head elevated 30°. No food or water (swallowing reflex may be absent). Note time of symptom onset (thrombolysis window is 4.5 hours). Call 108 IMMEDIATELY — "Time is brain"
ALL stroke — immediate emergency. Every minute of stroke = 1.9 million neurons lost. Do not give aspirin without CT (may be haemorrhagic)

cards.title: Minor Surgery & Wound Management — BNYS Scope

alerts.label: Scope of Practice Note
alerts.text: Minor surgical procedures in BNYS practice are limited to: wound cleaning and dressing, suture removal, simple wound closure (skin closures/steri-strips), blister management, ingrown toenail basic care, minor laceration dressing, and referral triage. Procedures requiring anaesthesia, incision, or suturing beyond simple closure require surgical referral. Always obtain informed consent and document all procedures.

rows: Clean laceration <2cm
Irrigate, steri-strips or tissue glue, non-adherent dressing
Deep structures involved, gaping
Check — update if >5 years or unknown
Bite wound (animal/human)
Irrigate PROFUSELY, do NOT close primarily, broad-spectrum antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate), check rabies risk
All deep bites, face, hands, immunocompromised
Always check — bites are dirty wounds
Puncture wound
Clean entrance, do NOT probe, observe for infection
Foot puncture (osteomyelitis risk), retained foreign body suspected
High-risk — update tetanus
Infected wound
Wound swab, debridement, silver dressing, systemic antibiotics if cellulitis extends >2cm
Spreading cellulitis, lymphangitis, systemic features, abscess requiring I&D
Check — infection slows healing

pearls.label: 🟡 Tetanus Prophylaxis Quick Guide
pearls.text: Clean wound + fully vaccinated (≥3 doses + booster within 10 years) = NO treatment needed. Clean wound + not fully vaccinated = Td vaccine. Dirty/tetanus-prone wound (contaminated, puncture, bite, necrotic) + fully vaccinated (within 5 years) = NO treatment. Dirty wound + not vaccinated/unknown = Td vaccine + human tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG) 250 IU IM (different sites). Tetanus-prone wounds: all wounds contaminated with soil/faeces, puncture wounds, animal bites, burns, wounds >6hrs old before cleaning.

steps.title: Wound Assessment
steps.text: ABCD of wounds: Area (size, depth, location), Bleeding (controlled/active), Contamination (clean/contaminated/infected/dirty), Delayed presentation (>6hrs = contaminated — do not primarily close). Assess: neurovascular status distal to wound, tendon integrity (ask patient to flex/extend against resistance), signs of infection (calor, rubor, dolor, tumor + pus, lymphangitis, cellulitis). Tetanus immunisation status.

steps.title: Wound Cleaning Protocol
steps.text: Irrigate copiously with normal saline under pressure (20ml syringe, 19G needle removed — irrigation not soaking reduces infection risk by 80%). Remove visible debris with forceps under good light. Debride necrotic tissue only if trained. Do not scrub wound (destroys viable tissue). Antiseptics (povidone-iodine, hydrogen peroxide) are CYTOTOXIC to healing tissue — use only in heavily contaminated wounds, not for clean wounds. Cover with non-adherent dressing.

steps.title: Wound Closure — When and How
steps.text: Primary closure (within 6hrs for clean wounds, 3hrs for face — excellent blood supply). Delayed primary closure (days 3–5 after contaminated wounds heal under observation). Secondary intention (infected, bite wounds, irregular margins — heal from bottom up). Skin closures/Steri-strips for: superficial wounds <2cm with good approximation. Tissue glue (Dermabond) for: facial wounds in children, linear wounds <4cm. Refer for: deep wounds, wounds crossing joints, wounds with tendon/bone/nerve exposure, face (cosmesis).

steps.title: Dressing Selection
steps.text: Dry wounds (no exudate): non-adherent dressing (Melolin, Telfa) + bandage. Moderate exudate: foam dressing (Allevyn, Mepilex). High exudate: alginate dressing (Kaltostat) — haemostatic properties. Infected wounds: silver-impregnated dressings (Mepilex Ag). Naturopathic wound adjuncts: honey (Medihoney — manuka, bacteriostatic ★★), aloe vera gel (anti-inflammatory, moisturising), calendula cream (anti-inflammatory, promotes granulation). Change dressings: infected = daily; clean healing = every 2–3 days.
